Summary of what was found: Sidel’s X-LITE Still shows industrialized ultra-lightweight PET in food and beverage: a 500 ml bottle weighing 6.5 g, with resin and energy savings versus a 12 g bottle.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: ultra-lightweight PET bottle design for non-pressurized beverages. Food/beverage use case: 500 ml still-water bottle. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: mass-market low-viscosity formats such as travel-size shampoo, body wash, micellar water, or refillable liquid-care bottles where PET is acceptable. Weight/material reduction relevance: 6.5 g versus 12 g directly evidences resin reduction potential. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market/value channels, not luxe. Compatibility/limitation notes: beverage-specific and does not prove suitability for oils, high alcohol, fragrances, pumps, or aggressive actives. Use in screening: keep as a top benchmark for rigid lightweight PET where cosmetic compatibility and handling requirements do not force thicker walls or complex dispensing systems.

Summary of what was found: ALPLA states plastic packaging weight has been reduced by an average of 25% since 1991 in Western Europe and reports a 1.5 L PET bottle reduced by 38% to 25 g.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight PET bottles at scale. Food/beverage use case: beverage bottles including a 1.5 L PET bottle reduced to 25 g. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: shampoo, conditioner, body wash, and personal-care bottles where PET clarity and blow-molding infrastructure already exist. Weight/material reduction relevance: quantified large-scale bottle-weight reduction and long-term industry trend. Premium versus mass-market fit: primarily mass-market; selected premium clear bottles possible where PET aesthetics are acceptable. Compatibility/limitation notes: does not address pumps, droppers, thick formulas, alcohol, or fragrance loss. Use in screening: use as evidence that lightweight PET is mature and scalable but requires formula, top-load, and closure-system screening.

Summary of what was found: ALPLA reports Coca-Cola India launched a packaged drinking water bottle made from 100% recycled PET, supporting rPET as a mature route with circularity relevance.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: 100% rPET bottle. Food/beverage use case: packaged drinking water in India. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: clear or lightly tinted personal-care bottles where recycled content and PET’s lower mass versus glass are desirable. Weight/material reduction relevance: does not give bottle grams but confirms full-rPET food-contact bottle commercialization. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market, with some premium use if appearance requirements are met. Compatibility/limitation notes: evidence of recycled-content readiness, not direct cosmetics compatibility or luxury appearance; beauty use needs color, odor, clarity, and scuff-resistance checks. Use in screening: keep rPET as a serious candidate wherever virgin PET is acceptable, especially mass-market personal care.
Source name: ALPLA supports Coca-Cola in launching India’s first 100% recycled PET Bottle for packaged drinking water — ALPLA Blog — https://blog.alpla.com/en/blog/products-innovation/alpla-supports-coca-cola-launching-indias-first-100-recycled-pet-bottle

Summary of what was found: Berry’s thin-wall dairy cups are injection molded in 100% PP and described as lightweight and durable, proving thin-wall PP as a food lightweighting route.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: thin-wall polypropylene containers. Food/beverage use case: dairy cups for cottage cheese, fresh mozzarella, and yogurt. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: cream jars, balms, mask tubs, refill pods, and lightweight tubs for mass beauty/personal care. Weight/material reduction relevance: thin-wall format reduces material through downgauging, aligned with material-based lightweighting scope. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market skincare and personal-care tubs; limited luxe use unless decoration or overpack elevates feel. Compatibility/limitation notes: does not prove performance with fragrant, oily, solvent-bearing, or active-rich formulas. Use in screening: keep thin-wall PP as a priority route for commodity jars/tubs and refill inserts where premium heft is not required.
Source name: Berry offers the latest in PP cups — Berry Global via Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/berry-offers-the-latest-in-pp-cups

Summary of what was found: Berry reports a tethered-cap collaboration with Coca-Cola Bottling Company United achieved a 10% closure weight reduction and made the overall pack around 20% lighter than the previous finish.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight beverage closure. Food/beverage use case: tethered cap for beverage bottles. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: lightweight disc-tops, flip-tops, screw caps, and non-dispensing closures for personal care. Weight/material reduction relevance: quantifies closure-level and full-pack reduction, important because closures are often overlooked in beauty lightweighting. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market high-volume bottles where small gram savings scale quickly. Compatibility/limitation notes: beverage closures are mechanically simpler than pumps, droppers, airless systems, or prestige actuators. Use in screening: create a standalone closure-lightweighting workstream rather than focusing only on container bodies.
Source name: Tethered Cap for Coca Cola Bottling Company United — Berry Global via Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/14276-berry-cocacola-collaborate-to-implement-tethered-c

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmPrima PE Plus coffee pack is a mono-material PE structure with high-barrier performance, line speeds comparable to non-recyclable materials, and up to 38% lower carbon footprint than standard packs.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material PE high-barrier flexible pouch. Food/beverage use case: coffee packaging. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: refill pouches, sachets, and low-profile flexible packs for shampoos, body wash, soaps, or powdered beauty products. Weight/material reduction relevance: replaces heavier rigid formats or harder-to-recycle multi-material laminates with a lighter mono-material structure. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market refills; selective premium use where soft-pack convenience is acceptable. Compatibility/limitation notes: coffee aroma/moisture barrier does not automatically prove suitability for oils, alcohols, fragrances, or active skincare. Use in screening: advance mono-PE as a leading lightweight route but segment cosmetics by formulation aggressiveness before extrapolating food-pack performance.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle Ready Packaging Solutions for coffee — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/afla/coffee

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s Stonyfield pouch moved refrigerated yogurt from a multi-laminate structure to an all-PE film with a spout while removing metallized/foil layers and retaining heat resistance, barrier, hermetic seals, and premium graphics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: all-PE spouted pouch. Food/beverage use case: refrigerated yogurt pouch. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: shampoo, baby care, body wash, cleanser, and liquid-soap refills or direct-use spouted packs. Weight/material reduction relevance: replacing multi-laminate structures with all-PE preserves pouch-level lightweighting and improves recyclability directionally. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market and family-size refills; some premium opportunity for modern refill systems. Compatibility/limitation notes: proves barrier, sealing, and production validation for yogurt, not direct compatibility with essential oils, alcohol-rich products, or aggressive surfactants. Use in screening: place all-PE spouted pouches near the top of the shortlist for refill-led beauty routes, subject to formula and fitment testing.
Source name: Amcor collaborates to launch first all-PE spouted pouch — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/media/news/amcor-stonyfield-cpna-spouted-pouch

Summary of what was found: Mondi says BarrierPack Recyclable is a fully recyclable all-PE high-barrier laminate that is lighter than PET/PE laminates of the same thickness, runs on existing lines, and is offered for food, pet food, home and personal care.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: all-PE high-barrier flexible laminate/pouch. Food/beverage use case: confectionery and food. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: refill pouches, stand-up pouches, shaped pouches, and flexible packs for home/personal care. Weight/material reduction relevance: source states the structure is lighter than PET/PE laminates of the same thickness. Premium versus mass-market fit: attractive for mass-market refills and scalable FMCG formats; limited premium use where soft packs are acceptable. Compatibility/limitation notes: barrier and line compatibility are supported, but beauty formulas still require chemical compatibility, odor, and stress-testing work. Use in screening: treat as one of the strongest transferable routes because the supplier explicitly spans food through home/personal care.
Source name: Mondi’s BarrierPack Recyclable helps consumer goods brands advance their circular economy goals — Mondi Group — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2020/mondis-barrierpack-recyclable-helps-consumer-goods-brands-advance-their-circular-economy-goals/

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s home and personal care page shows the AmPrima PE Plus family already positioned for personal care, with up to 54% lower carbon footprint than standard packs plus high clarity/transparency for shelf appeal.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-PE recycle-ready flexible pouch for home and personal care. Food/beverage use case: cross-over proof from food-style mono-PE flexible platforms into personal care. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: body wash, shampoo, soap, personal-care refills, and flexible refill formats. Weight/material reduction relevance: characterizes pouches as lightweight and easy to carry/store. Premium versus mass-market fit: clearest for mass-market; selected premium brands could use it where refill systems matter more than rigid heft. Compatibility/limitation notes: does not prove suitability for all beauty categories such as prestige skincare with high sensory expectations or solvent-sensitive formulas. Use in screening: evidence that mono-PE flexible routes have already moved from food-style technology platforms into personal care at scale.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for home and personal care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/afla/home-and-personal-care

Summary of what was found: Tetra Pak says a paper-based barrier can replace the aluminum-foil layer in aseptic cartons while maintaining comparable food protection and shelf life; one example reached 87% renewable content and 26% package carbon-footprint reduction.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: paper-based barrier in carton packaging replacing aluminum foil. Food/beverage use case: aseptic soy-milk carton on high-speed filling equipment. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: niche opportunities in refill cartons, low-viscosity liquid refills, or paper-barrier workstreams. Weight/material reduction relevance: simplifies material structure directionally and cuts package footprint in the cited example. Premium versus mass-market fit: more realistic for mass-market refill concepts than premium main packs. Compatibility/limitation notes: complex barrier structure built for aseptic liquid food and specialized filling systems; not a near-term mainstream replacement for bottles, jars, or pump packs. Use in screening: keep on the longlist for refill/carton concepts, but filter out early for most mainstream cosmetic primary packaging.
Source name: Tetra Pak extends paper-based barrier packaging to high-speed lines in Asia — Tetra Pak — https://www.tetrapak.com/en-in/about-tetra-pak/news-and-events/newsarchive/tetra-pak-extends-paper-based-barrier-to-high-speed-lines

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmFiber Performance Paper is used for dry-food formats such as confectionery, bars, cookies, cereals, dry beverages, and dry mixes, and runs on existing flexible-packaging equipment.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: paper-based high-barrier flexible packaging. Food/beverage use case: dry-food formats such as bars, cereals, cookies, and dry-mix sachets. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: powder products, dry sample sachets, sheet-mask overwraps, or selective secondary/overwrap applications. Weight/material reduction relevance: materially light flexible paper structures can replace some plastic-heavy small-format wraps. Premium versus mass-market fit: mostly mass-market or niche eco-positioned brands; less suited to luxe unless used as secondary component with strong decoration. Compatibility/limitation notes: evidence should not be stretched to oily, wet, alcohol-rich, or fragrance-loaded cosmetics without separate validation. Use in screening: advance only for dry-product beauty formats and selective paper-overwrap formats, not mainstream wet primary packs.
Source name: High-Barrier Performance Paper Packaging Launches in North America — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/media/news/paper-packaging-launch-north-america

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso says FiberLight Tec creates lighter boards with strength and stiffness using less raw material; Performa Light folding carton is more than 5% lighter than without the treatment.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight folding-carton paperboard. Food/beverage use case: premium food folding carton. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: secondary packaging such as folding cartons, sleeves, gift cartons, and premium paperboard outer packs. Weight/material reduction relevance: quantified board weight reduction while maintaining strength and printability. Premium versus mass-market fit: suitable for both, especially premium/luxe beauty where tactile quality, stiffness, and print surface matter. Compatibility/limitation notes: secondary-pack transfer route, not a primary wet-contact container solution. Use in screening: include as a key secondary-pack route because it addresses material-based lightweighting without changing pack shape or footprint.
Source name: Light-weight materials, efficient production – How technological innovation shapes the future of food packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/newsroom/news/2024/7/technological-innovation-shapes-the-future-of-food-packaging

Summary of what was found: Huhtamaki’s blueloop portfolio offers mono-material packaging in paper, PE, PP, and PET across food, pet food, beverages, home and personal care, and healthcare while aiming to minimize material use and improve recyclability.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: mono-material flexible packaging platforms in paper, PE, PP, and PET. Food/beverage use case: food and beverage flexible packs. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: home and personal care flexible packs, sachets, pouches, and refill structures. Weight/material reduction relevance: source frames solutions as minimizing material use while advancing recyclability. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for mass-market FMCG, with selected premium refill applications possible. Compatibility/limitation notes: portfolio-level source proves platform breadth rather than a single detailed specification. Use in screening: justify including mono-material PE/PP/PET/paper flexible structures in the longlist for refill and sachet workstreams.

Summary of what was found: Huhtamaki’s foodservice range shows molded fiber, fluted fiber packaging, and folding cartons as lightweight fiber routes, with fluted packaging also listed for cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: molded fiber, fluted fiber packaging, and folding cartons. Food/beverage use case: foodservice plates, bowls, trays, boxes, and beverage-related lids/containers. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: molded fiber inserts, trays, lids, fluted protective structures, and lightweight fiber-based secondary packaging. Weight/material reduction relevance: fiber structures can replace heavier plastic inserts or some mixed-material secondary components. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest in mass-market and eco-positioned beauty; selective premium use possible for molded inserts or high-design fiber packs. Compatibility/limitation notes: molded fiber should still be treated mainly as secondary/tertiary or dry-contact packaging unless coating and formulation testing are added. Use in screening: bring forward molded fiber, fluted fiber, and folding-carton routes for secondary/tertiary packaging, not mainstream wet-fill primary containers.

Summary of what was found: International Paper’s wine-shipping page says corrugated cushioning, molded fiber inserts, and die-cut dividers protect bottles while engineers aim to minimize total package weight.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: corrugated cushioning, molded fiber inserts, and die-cut dividers. Food/beverage use case: wine bottle shipping protection. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: tertiary and e-commerce packaging for glass perfume bottles, serum bottles, gift sets, and premium skincare kits. Weight/material reduction relevance: explicitly links protective insert design with minimizing total package weight. Premium versus mass-market fit: relevant to both, especially premium/luxe glass-heavy SKUs. Compatibility/limitation notes: transit protection source, not shelf packaging or direct product contact. Use in screening: prioritize molded-fiber and corrugated-insert routes to replace heavier foam/plastic protection in beauty e-commerce.
Source name: Wine Packaging Solutions | Recyclable Wine Shipping Boxes — International Paper — https://www.internationalpaper.com/packaging/corrugated-packaging/wine-packaging

Summary of what was found: Pregis’ EverTec cushioned mailer is a two-layer paper mailer, lists cosmetics among ideal applications, and is positioned as a lightweight small-box replacement with typical weights from 0.54 oz to 1.98 oz.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: paper cushioned mailer for parcel/e-commerce. Food/beverage use case: functionally analogous to light e-commerce shipping of smaller consumer goods. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: shipping cosmetics, accessories, samples, and selected low-fragility beauty products. Weight/material reduction relevance: provides actual mailer weights and positions the format as a box replacement that saves freight and labor. Premium versus mass-market fit: useful for both, especially direct-to-consumer channels. Compatibility/limitation notes: not appropriate for products too heavy or fragile for mailers; glass-heavy prestige sets may still need corrugated shipper plus inserts. Use in screening: evaluate paper cushioned mailers as a tertiary lightweighting route for smaller or less-breakable beauty items.
Source name: EverTec Cushioned Mailer | Durable & Recyclable Protection — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/us-solutions/by-product/shipping-mailers/cushioned-evertec/

Summary of what was found: Ball’s Alumi-Tek beverage bottle is described as a lightweight, recyclable aluminum package for a wide variety of beverages, supporting aluminum as a premium/differentiation-led transfer route.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight aluminum bottle. Food/beverage use case: reclosable aluminum beverage bottle. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: premium shampoo, body care, deodorant, haircare, fragrance-adjacent, or refillable liquid formats where metal look-and-feel adds value. Weight/material reduction relevance: source calls the package lightweight and aluminum offers a non-glass premium route. Premium versus mass-market fit: better for premium/luxe or hero SKUs than broad mass-market due to converting, lining, and decoration complexity. Compatibility/limitation notes: transfer by inference from beverage use; does not prove all cosmetic formulas are compatible, so lacquer/lining, denting, closure fit, and sourcing economics need separate screening. Use in screening: keep aluminum as a premium-track route where brands want to replace heavier glass while preserving prestige cues.
Source name: Ball to Manufacture Reclosable Alumi-Tek Aluminum Beverage Bottles — Ball — https://www.ball.com/newswire/article/123701/ball-to-manufacture-reclosable-alumi-tek-aluminum-beverage-bottles

Summary of what was found: PETCORE Europe says perforated sleeves on household and personal care PET bottles are only compatible with recycling if the bottle remains recognizable as PET and the sleeve/ink system separates correctly without ink bleed.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: sleeve/label design constraints on PET bottle recyclability. Food/beverage use case: PET-bottle recycling logic transferable from beverage-style PET systems. Possible cosmetic/personal care transfer: lightweight PET/rPET cosmetic bottles with body sleeves or high-decoration label systems. Weight/material reduction relevance: lightweighting gains can be undermined if label/sleeve architecture blocks sorting and recycling, so the full material system matters. Premium versus mass-market fit: relevant to both, especially decorated premium packs relying on full-body sleeves. Compatibility/limitation notes: recyclability rather than grams-saved source; value is as a screening constraint. Use in screening: treat sleeve material, inks, and wash-off/separation performance as part of the transferability checklist for PET/rPET routes.
Source name: Recyclability of Household and Personal Care PET bottles - Perforated Sleeves Approved — PETCORE Europe — https://www.petcore-europe.org/news-events/218-recyclability-of-household-and-personal-care-pet-bottles-perforated-sleeves-approved.html
